title: Preparation and cryopreservation of human liver samples  for analysis by flow cytometry (fresh or after cryobanking)
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"2gdv7","text":"This protocol focuses on the preparation of small human liver samples for single-cell analysis by flow cytometry. It provides two options for sample preparation: (1) immediate flow cytometry with fresh samples or (2) cryopreservation of samples at -80°C using the Stable-Lyse/Store V2 system from SmartTube Inc. for flow cytometry at a later timepoint.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":50,"length":20},{"style":"bold","offset":196,"length":6},{"style":"bold","offset":217,"length":17},{"style":"bold","offset":264,"length":20}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"191pc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"93emu","text":"Note: This protocol does not provide guidelines on how to create a multicolor flow cytometry panel!","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"24gm4","text":"This protocol is validated for immune cell isolation of human liver and tumor specimen (weight 150-1000 mg). It has not yet been tested on biopsy material (\u003c 20 mg).","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":30,"length":22},{"style":"bold","offset":56,"length":30}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1c1fo","text":"The retrieval of parenchymal cells is a by-product of this protocol but it has not been optimized for this purpose!","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":17,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7kead","text":"Experiments should be performed on ice at all times!","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":32,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7i95u","text":"Work with filter tips!","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":10,"length":11}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vbf2","text":"When working with fluorochrome-conjugated antibodies, avoid bright (sun) light and consider using aluminum foil to protect your samples from light while incubating.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":18,"length":34}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1q1in","text":"For waste management and time reasons, transfer of e.g. larger liquid volumes is done by pouring it directly from the bottle. If you prefer otherwise, you can always use serological pipets.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":33}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7b9qo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ftval","text":"Advantages:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":10}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"etqc3","text":"Isolation of all major immune cell subtypes including granulocytes.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":54,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fk2v","text":"Nycodenz gradient ensures minimal amount of parenchymal cells which improves the quality of the FACS analysis.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"btn5","text":"Ready-to-use quality-controlled cryo-preservation buffers.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"1n1l1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3nrat","text":"Limitations:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":12}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7u3fs","text":"As mentioned above, this protocol has not been tested on biopsies (\u003c 20 mg tissue).","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":38,"length":15},{"style":"bold","offset":57,"length":8}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6gbt1","text":"If you also focus on isolation of hepatocytes, two-step-collagenase perfusion of bigger liver specimen might be a more suitable approach.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":21,"length":24}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2s5ie","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"24vgt","text":"Qualifications:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5a56d","text":"We recommend basic experience in wet-lab work (e.g. how to pipette) to handle this protocol. You also might need a trial run  to familiarize yourself with the procedures.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":13,"length":32}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"f2g7o","text":"Make sure you read the safety warnings regarding untested human samples!","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":49,"length":22}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"57a9q","text":"Make sure an ethics approval has been obtained before working with subjects or patient samples!","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":13,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8cfat","text":"Revisit the materials list to make sure all the equipment, materials and reagents are available to you.","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":12,"length":15}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
warning: {"blocks":[{"key":"d82vc","text":"If your work with untested human samples (e.g. status of Hepatitis B/C and HIV is unknown), all experiments must be carried out in a Class II biosafety cabinet (with laminar air flow). Liquid waste has to be collected and autoclaved before disposal, contaminated materials have to be collected and disposed of separately from other lab waste as they are potentially infectious.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":18,"length":23},{"style":"bold","offset":46,"length":1},{"style":"bold","offset":133,"length":50},{"style":"bold","offset":222,"length":10},{"style":"bold","offset":297,"length":23}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"eg98r","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7qaql","text":"Use caps/lids to close your FACS tube when removing them from the biosafety cabinet for centrifugation. Use pipet tips with filters.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":4,"length":9},{"style":"bold","offset":28,"length":9},{"style":"bold","offset":124,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"dikjm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"49s69","text":"Depending on your personal safety preferences as well as the specific regulations of your research department/institute/country, there might be other or additional regulations to consider. Contact your biosafety officer! ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":202,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Start the biosafety cabinet.
Prepare all the reagents and equipment needed for cell isolation.

Place all needed buffers and reagents , including a 50 ml falcon tube containing HBSS , On ice or in the wasterbath. 2x HBSS in 50 ml Falcon (4°C and 37 °C) Stop Digest Buffer (on ice) HBSS +0.1% BSA 1x Lysis Buffer FACS buffer (when you start FACS right after isolation) Blocking Buffer
Place urin cups filled with around 50 ml Medium (RPMI) for your samples On ice .

Freshly weigh Collagenase B ( 8.52 mg per sample) and DNase I ( 1.875 mg per sample) in 2 ml tubes and place them On ice until further use. You may prefer to predilute your enzymes and freeze them in aliquots . We choose to prepare the enzymes freshly for stable enzyme activity .
Prechill a centrifuge to 4 °C as well as preheat a (shaking) waterbath to 37 °C . Place a 50 ml falcon tube containing HBSS inside the waterbath to warm up.

Set up the biosafety cabinet with all the equipment you'll need and make sure, everything you will need can be reached easily. Preparing all the equipment you need will save you ample time. Working fast is key in recovering viable liver/tumor immune cells.
Directly before leaving for the OR to pick up the samples, place the Collagenase in the waterbath to preheat.

Place the liver samples in the urine cups with medium, and transport them back to the lab On ice . Place samples (still on ice) inside the biosafety cabinet. CAVE - With the exception of the digestion, which takes place at 37 °C , place your samples on ice at all times!

Dissolve the DNase I in an appropriate amount of cold HBSS ( 7.5 mL per sample) and keep On ice in a 15 ml Falcon. HOW TO - Using a 1000 µl pipette, dissolve the DNase in 1 ml cold HBSS and transfer to a 15 ml falcon tube. Add remaining amoutn of cold HBSS and mix well.
Dissolve Collagenase B in an appropriate amount of 37 C° warm HBSS ( 3 mL per sample) in a 15 ml Falcon. Transfer back to 37 °C until needed for digestion.
Weigh the tissue samples and place in petri dishes on a cooled sample holder.
Cut sample into very small pieces using first a scapel and then fine scissors until the whole tissue appears pulpous .

Add 3 mL Collagenase B to each petri dish, swirl to mix and transfer to a 50 ml falcon by pouring the liquid directly into the tube. Use a small forceps to get all the tissue pieces out of the petri dish.

Immediately transfer to a shaking waterbath ( 37 °C , 150 rpm, °C, ) and digest for 00:30:00 . Shake the sample vigorously every 5 min! CAVE - If you do not have a shaking waterbath, any waterbath will do. However, it is crucial to shake the sample on a regular basis!
Using the same 10 ml serological pipet, filter the sample through a 100 µm cell strainer into a new 50 ml falcon.

Use 20 mL cold Digest Stop to rinse the old falcon. Gently press the remaining tissue through the cell strainer with the smooth end of a syringe plunger , repeatingly rinsing the mesh with the Digest Stop from the old falcon. CAVE - Press the plunger straight down on the cell strainer and release in tapping motions.

If you want to perform lysis right away, continue here. Otherwise, continue with step 21 . GOOD TO KNOW - The gradient will also eliminate erythrocytes - if you do not plan to work with the parenchymal cells, you can skip this step. You can also treat parenchymal cells with lysis buffer at a later timepoint or use an erythrocyte depletion kit.
Remove the cell strainer and add 3 mL DNase I to the cell suspension.
Place your falcons back On ice and discard the supernatant by pouring it into the liquid waste bottle without disrupting the pellet.

Spin 50 x g, 4 °C, 2 minutes to pellet the parenchymal cells. If you set focus on isolating parenchymal cells (hepatocytes), you might extent this step to 00:05:00 - keep in mind that you might lose some of your non-parenchymal cells when you do so. Furthermore, fractions of parenchymal cells will be relatively small, as the tissue section is cut and digested without perfusion.
Transfer the supernatant to a new falcon and spin 500 x g, 4 °C, 10 minutes .
Discard the supernatant and add cold HBSS + 0,1% BSA with a serological pipet to a final volume of 7.4 mL and gently resuspend.
Add 12.6 mL Nycodenz stock solution with a serological pipet and gently mix until homogenous.

Overlay the Nycodenz-Cell Suspension with 6 mL HBSS + 0,1% BSA. HOW TO OVERLAY - Due to the higher density of Nycodenz, HBSS will layer on top if it is applied carefully. 1. The dispension force of your pipette controller has to be deactived. 2. Hold the 50 ml falcon containing the Nycodenz-cell suspension at a 45° angle . 3. Aspirate 6 ml of HBSS into a 10 ml serological pipet and press the tip to the tube wall in a 90° angle (the tip opening should be flat on the tube wall). 4. Very slightly press the dispense button and let the HBSS run down the side of the tube in a constant trickle without any drops forming.
Transfer the falcon tubes to a centrifuge and spin 1400 x g, 4 °C, 22 minutes without break (takes around 45min total).

After centrifugation, place the tubes back On ice . Carefully remove the first 2-3 ml from the top layer (debris and dead cells) and discard. Next, use a 10 ml serological pipet to slowly remove the white interphase (middle layer containing the immune cells ) and transfer to a new falcon tube. You can also use a sterile pasteur pipet to remove the middle layer.
Add cold HBSS + 0,1% BSA to a final volume of 30 mL and spin 500 x g, 4 °C, 10 minutes .
Discard the supernatant without disrupting the pellet and resuspend the cells in 2 mL cold FACS buffer .

Pass the cell suspension through a 35 µm cell strainer cap into FACS tubes placed On ice . Discard the cell strainer cap afterwards. Always close the FACS tubes with a cap before taking them out of the biosafety cabinet!
Spin 500 x g, 4 °C, 5 minutes
Depending on whether you want to perform flow cytometry on fresh or cryo-preserved samples, you can select different protocol options at this step.

Label your 5 ml FACS tubes and place them in a cold sample holder or On ice . You might need several tubes per sample, depending on the number of panels and controls you are planning to run. GOOD TO KNOW - As liver samples are very autofluorescent, recording an unstained control for every sample will give you a far better unmixing/compensation result. FMO (fluorescence minus one) controls are helpful to discriminate positive and negative (unspecific background fluorescence) signals, especially if the positive cell populations are not distinctly separated from the negative population. In an FMO tube, all fluorochromes in the panel are present except the fluorochrome in question.

Prepare the antibody mix: For each FACS tube, use 20 µL blocking buffer and the appropriate amount of each of your antibodies . Always prepare the master mix for one extra sample or add 10% to account for pipetting errors. EXAMPLE 1 2 samples (one full stain and one control each) + 1 extra for pipetting error = 5x mix 20 antibodies in panel (1µl needed per sample) Blocking Buffer: 5 x 20 µl = 100 µl 5 µl per antibody final volume of antibody master mix: 200 µl mix to add per FACS tube = 20 µl blocking buffer + 20 µl antibodies = 40 µl EXAMPLE 2 2 samples (one full stain and one control each) = 4x mix +10% 20 antibodies in panel (1µl needed per sample) Blocking Buffer: 4x 20 µl = 80 µl (+ 10%) = 88 µl Each antibody: 4 µl per antibody +10% = 4,4 µl per antibody final volume of antibody master mix: 176 µl mix to add per FACS tube = 20 µl blocking buffer + 20 µl antibodies = 40 µl

Divide the cell suspension between the different FACS tubes. Its important to document the amount of sample going into each tube to allow determination of absolute cell numbers during analysis (eg., calculate cells/ gram tissue ).

Discard the supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in the remaining buffer by flicking the tube. HOW TO DECANT - Empty the supernatant with momentum and - while keeping the FACS tube in an upside-down position to not disrupt the pellet on the bottom - dip the tube onto a paper towel to get rid of the excess liquid. Turn the FACS tube upright and gently flick to resuspend.
Bring the volume in each FACS tube to 100 µL with FACS buffer . Set aside the unstained control.

Add the appropriate concentration of fixable viability stain to the cell suspension in each of the FACS tubes and mix well by flicking the tube. HOW TO "FLICK" - Mix well by holding the upper part of the tube between thumb and index finger of one hand and at the same time, gently flick the bottom of the tube repeatedly with the index finger of the other hand.
Incubate for 00:05:00 On ice .

Add the appropriate amount of the prepared antibody mix to each tube. Gently pipette up and down to mix. EXPERT TIP - In case you are using FMO control(s), you can prepare the antibody mix for all your tubes without the FMO antibody(ies) and add them later to your full stain tube.
Incubate for 00:20:00 On ice .
If you prefer, switch of the light in the biosafety cabinet/lamina flow hood to avoid bleaching the fluorochrome-conjugated antibodies.
Add 2 mL FACS buffer and mix well by pipetting up and down.
Spin 500 x g, 4 °C, 5 minutes
Discard the supernatant by decanting and resuspend the cell pellet in the remaining buffer by flicking the tube.
Add 1 mL Fixation Buffer and mix well by pipetting up and down.
Incubate for 00:10:00 at On ice .
Add 2 mL FACS buffer and mix well by pipetting up and down. After fixation, samples can be further processed on the bench, a biosafety cabinet is no longer necessary.
Spin 500 x g, 4 °C, 5 minutes
Discard the supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in the remaining buffer by flicking the tube.

Add 200 µL FACS buffer and proceed to sample aquisition. Fixed samples can be stored at 4 °C overnight and recorded the next day. However, keep in mind that fixatives such as formalin can change the autofluorescence of your cells and destabilize fluorochromes, especially tandem dyes. If possible, record you samples directly after fixation.

Label 5 ml FACS tubes and 1,8 ml cryotubes to store your samples. Remember to prepare additional aliquots to allow for staining controls at analysis (such as FMO or unstained control). GOOD TO KNOW - Long-term fixation and storage might considerably change the autofluorescence spectra of your cells of interest. Therefore, it is recommended to include at least one extra aliquot for an unstained control to optimize the quality of your flow cytometry data.
Divide the cell suspension between the FACS tubes. Its important to document how you split up the sample because you will need this later to calculate absolute cell numbers (cells gram tissue).

Using a small pipett, add the appropriate concentration of fixable viability stain to the FACS tubes containing full stain and FMO sample and mix well by flicking the tube. Do NOT add fixable viability stain to the unstained control .
Bring the volume in each FACS tube to 250 µL with FACS buffer . Set aside the unstained control.
Incubate for 00:05:00 On ice .

After incubation, add 2 mL FACS buffer to each tube and mix well by pipetting up and down. GOOD TO KNOW - After incubation, the remaining fixable viability stain should be diluted and washed off. If you leave it in your sample, it will bind to your fixed cells and more or less all cells will stain positive.
Spin 500 x g, 4 °C, 5 minutes .
Using a 1000 µl pipette, carefully pipette off 2 mL of the supernatant without disrupting the cell pellet on the bottom of the tube.
Gently mix the remaining volume (it should be approximately 250 µL ) and transfer to the corresponding 1,8 ml cryotube .
Add 350 µL Stable-Lyse Buffer to each cryotube, mix well by pipetting up and down.
Incubate 00:15:00 at Room temperature .
Add 1000 µL Stable-Store Buffer to each cryotube, mix well by pipetting up and down.
Close the cryotubes and incubate 00:15:00 at Room temperature . After fixation, samples can be further processed on the bench, a biosafety cabinet is no longer necessary.
Immediately transfer to -80 °C for long-term storage. Samples can be stored for several months.
Transfer your samples from -80 °C to an open rack to the fridge ( 4 °C ) to thaw for approximately 01:00:00 .

In the meantime, prepare the antibody mix : For each FACS tube, use 20 µL blocking buffer and the appropriate amount of each of your antibodies . Always prepare the master mix for one extra sample or add 10% for pipetting errors. EXAMPLE 1 2 samples (one full stain and one control each) + 1 extra for pipetting error = 5x mix 20 antibodies in panel (1µl needed per sample) Blocking Buffer: 5 x 20 µl = 100 µl 5 µl per antibody final volume of antibody master mix: 200 µl mix to add per FACS tube = 20 µl blocking buffer + 20 µl antibodies = 40 µl EXAMPLE 2 2 samples (one full stain and one control each) = 4x mix +10% 20 antibodies in panel (1µl needed per sample) Blocking Buffer: 4x 20 µl = 80 µl (+ 10%) = 88 µl Each antibody: 4 µl per antibody +10% = 4,4 µl per antibody final volume of antibody master mix: 176 µl mix to add per FACS tube = 20 µl blocking buffer + 20 µl antibodies = 40 µl
Add 2 mL FACS buffer to each tube and mix well by pipetting up and down.
Discard the supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in the remaining buffer by flicking the tube.
Spin 500 x g, Room temperature, 5 minutes
go to step #51 and repeat wash-spin-discard supernatant . Afterwards follow-up with step 55 .
Bring each sample to a final volume of 100 µL by filling up the missing amount with FACS buffer.

Add the appropriate amount of the prepared antibody mix to each tube. Gently pipette up and down to mix. EXPERT TIP - In case you are using FMO control(s), you can prepare the antibody mix for all your tubes without the FMO antibody(ies) and add them later to your full stain tube.
Incubate 00:20:00 at Room temperature .
Add 2 mL FACS buffer to each tube.
Spin 500 x g, Room temperature, 5 minutes
Discard the supernatant and resuspend the cell pellet in the remaining buffer by flicking the tube.
Add 200 µL FACS buffer and proceed to sample aquisition.

If your samples are very bloody, you may attempt to flush the samples. For this, fill a 10 ml syringe with cold HBSS and attach a needle . Puncture the sample and carefully press the HBSS into the tissue to flush out the blood. Repeat this process several times until the liquid runs mostly clear. Discard the liquid before proceeding.
Add 250 µL DNase I to each falcon and close the top tightly. Mix by swirling the falcon
After digestion, place falcons On ice and immediately add 10 mL cold Digest Stop to end the digestion process.

Use a 10 ml serological pipet to pipette the cell suspension up and down multiple times to further detach the cells from their connective tissue. When the cell suspension runs easily through the pipet, proceed to the next step.
Add 40 mL cold Digest Stop and 4 mL DNase I to each falcon.
Resuspend the cells by gently flicking the tube or pipetting up and down. Add 2 mL 1X Lysis Buffer , gently pipette up and down to mix and incubate for 00:05:00 On ice .
Spin 500 x g, 4 °C, 5 minutes .

Prepare Nycodenz stock solution ( 14ml per sample) and place On ice . per sample weigh 4g of Nycodenz in a 50 ml Falcon add 14 ml of GBSS to the Falcon close the top tightly and vortex until half of the Nycodenz has dissolved let the falcon rest on the side on the countertop for 5 min vortex again until dissolved completely keep on ice
Transfer the thawed samples to their corresponding 5 ml FACS tubes .
creator: Wiebke Werner
